procedure.description: ACDF C5-C6 AND C6-C7, two levels
procedure.requested_by: Dr. James Wilson, MD — Spine Surgery

denial.reason_text: Two-level ACDF not medically necessary. Imaging shows pathology at C5-C6 only. C6-C7 shows mild degenerative changes without significant compression or neurological deficit attributable to this level.

appeal_basis: Member meets all plan criteria for at least the C5-C6 level
Surgeon believes both levels should be addressed to prevent adjacent segment disease
6 weeks PT completed, pharmacotherapy and injection done

clinical_record.presenting_diagnosis: Cervical radiculopathy C5-C6 with adjacent C6-C7 degenerative disease

mri_1.findings: C5-C6: large disc herniation with moderate-severe nerve root compression, moderate foraminal stenosis. Clear correlation with C6 radicular symptoms. C6-C7: mild disc bulge, minimal foraminal narrowing, no cord contact, no nerve root compression identifiable.

neurological_exam.findings: Right C6 dermatomal numbness and tingling, bicep reflex diminished right, grip strength 4/5 right. No C7 findings documented. No bilateral findings. No upper motor neuron signs.

physical_therapy_summary.note: 8 weeks PT across two providers. C6 radicular symptoms persist. C7 symptoms were never documented.

right_c6_epidural.response: 50% temporary relief of right C6 symptoms, fully returned by week 4

clinical_record.surgeon_note: Dr. Wilson: I recommend two-level fusion to address both C5-C6 pathology and prophylactically stabilize C6-C7 to prevent adjacent segment disease, which has a 25% 5-year incidence after single-level ACDF.

question: Should the prior authorization denial for two-level ACDF C5-C6 and C6-C7 (CPT 22551 x2) be overturned on appeal?

ground_truth_complexity.obvious_reading: OVERTURN — member clearly meets all plan criteria: 8 weeks PT, two pharmacotherapy agents, injection, confirmed radiculopathy, imaging correlation.
ground_truth_complexity.harder_question_1: The plan criteria are met for C5-C6. Are they met for C6-C7 when imaging shows only mild changes and there are no documented C7 symptoms?
ground_truth_complexity.harder_question_2: Does prophylactic fusion of an asymptomatic level with mild imaging changes meet medical necessity standards?
ground_truth_complexity.harder_question_3: The clinical standard requires imaging AND symptoms to correspond to each fused level — does C6-C7 meet this standard?
ground_truth_complexity.harder_question_4: Adjacent segment disease prevention is a legitimate surgical consideration, but does it constitute medical necessity for the second level?
ground_truth_complexity.the_genuine_tension: Plan criteria clearly met for the overall procedure. A single-pass system reads criteria met and OVERTURNs both levels. But the plan denied the C6-C7 level specifically because there are no symptoms and minimal imaging findings at that level. Clinical standards require symptom-imaging correlation for each fused level. The surgeon's prophylactic argument doesn't meet the medical necessity threshold for the second level.
ground_truth_complexity.right_answer: PARTIAL — OVERTURN for C5-C6 (criteria met, symptoms documented, imaging correlation confirmed). UPHOLD for C6-C7 (no documented C7 symptoms, minimal imaging findings, prophylactic fusion alone does not meet medical necessity standard). Correct answer is PARTIAL, not full OVERTURN.
